segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
SINEAD BOVELL: Hi, everyone!

Well, it is such a
privilege to introduce

somebody whose work is shaping
the future of computing.

So Julian Kelly is
the senior director

of quantum hardware at Google
Quantum AI, where he brings

over a decade of expertise in
designing, and fabricating,

and fine-tuning semiconducting
quantum processors

from many generations
of Sycamore

to the latest
breakthrough Willow Chip.

Julian is one of the earliest
members of Google Quantum AI.
So welcome, Julian.
It's great to be here with you.
JULIAN KELLY: Thank you so much.
Really wonderful to be here.

SINEAD BOVELL: And we will
be doing Q&A at the end.

So if you do have
questions, you can save them
for the last 10 minutes.
OK.

So I'm going to start with a
question I'm curious about.

For the past 80 years,
the world has been
running on classical computers.

And we've done some pretty
remarkable things, arguably.

I mean, everything we're
talking about in AI, that's
powered by classical computers.

We've gone to space a few
times on classical computers.

Yet we consistently hear
that quantum computers

will be the successor
to classical computers.

So could you explain
the core concepts
behind quantum computing?

How is it different from
classical computers?

And why is quantum
computing important?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.
Thanks.
This is a great question.

So classical computers can solve
all these wonderful problems
as you just described.

But it turns out there are
some problems that only
a quantum computer can solve.

So a quantum computer can
solve certain problems

that would otherwise
be completely

impossible for a
classical computer.

In terms of the kind of
fundamentals of how they work,

classical computers
work on binary or bits.
That's in 0 and 1.

You can think of it as a
light switch that's either
in the on or the off position.

But qubits use quantum
bits or qubits.

And qubits can be in what are
called superposition states, 0
and 1 at the same time.

And they have this
exponential scaling behavior.

So every time you add
a qubit to your system,

you can think of that as
doubling the computational power
of your quantum computer.

Fundamentally,
quantum computers are

able to run on
quantum mechanics.

And quantum mechanics is what
underlies all the physical laws
of the world.

So quantum computers,
in some sense,

speak the natural
language of the universe.
SINEAD BOVELL: OK.

And we're going to get into
that a little bit more.

So would you consider
quantum computing then

to be the next big thing after
AI or is it more nuanced?

Will quantum and AI
converge in some way

and end up reinforcing
each other?
JULIAN KELLY: Yes.
It's a great question.

I think it's a good mental
model that quantum computing is
the next big thing after AI.
It's distinct from it.

But at the same time, they
do have the opportunity
to complement each other.

You can maybe think
of that in two ways.

AI helps us with
our day to day jobs

and building a quantum
computer right now.

But also a quantum computer
has the opportunity
to enhance AI systems as well.

SINEAD BOVELL: And we're
going to get into some
of the challenges with quantum.

But I want to talk about
some of the possibilities.

So why should we all
be paying attention
to what's happening in quantum?

What could theoretically
become possible in a world
with quantum computers?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

So there are a number
of application areas

that we've identified that
quantum could be beneficial in.

So for example, a
quantum computer

could be critical in the
drug discovery process.

So for example, we have a
paper from a few years ago

where we worked on
this particular enzyme.

It's called cytochrome
P450 enzyme.

And you could imagine if
you're a drug maker, one

of the things you do is you make
all these different candidate
drugs.

You work hard to
build all of them.

And then you don't
know how they're

going to behave when they
go inside the human body.

Some of them get
metabolized by this enzyme

and immediately
don't work right.

And the problem is
you can't predict

which ones are going to work or
which ones aren't, because you

would have to then understand
what quantum mechanics is
going to do.

So a quantum computer would
allow you to basically figure

out which drugs would or
would not be effective
beforehand in simulation.

And you wouldn't have
to spend your time

building all these candidate
drugs for a number of them
to immediately fail.

You could just focus
on the ones that

are going to be more
effective, which will just save
time and money for everyone.

SINEAD BOVELL: And
I'm starting to sense

some of that
convergence with what's

happening in AI
and drug discovery,

and then eventually being able
to model that with quantum.
OK.

So based on where the industry
stands in this moment, when do

you think quantum
computers will actually be
solving some of these problems?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.
So that's a big goal for us.

And we're optimistic that on the
time scale of about five years

or so, we could be able to solve
a real world interesting problem

on a quantum computer that
would be impossible to solve
on a classical computer.

SINEAD BOVELL: So just after
2030, we'll give you a call

and make sure it's
all ready to go.
OK.

So about a year
ago, I was attending
a conversation-- a conference.

And I struck up a
conversation with a professor
of quantum mechanics.

And he said something
that I will never forget.

He said, the more you learn
about quantum mechanics,
the less sense any of it makes.

So in terms of the
breakthroughs that we're

waiting for and
actually building

scalable, practical
computers, is it
breakthroughs in the hardware?
Is it the software?

Or is it understanding
of the strange world
of quantum physics?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

So there's maybe two
parts to your question.

The first one, I think it's so
interesting that quantum often

gets this, oh,
it's totally crazy.
It's unapproachable.

How could you really
understand it?

And I think what is so fun
about working on this project,

and one of the things
that really drew me in

is the fact that you really get
to interact with quantum systems
yourself.

When you're a student in
undergrad or graduate school,

you run all these
quantum equations.

And it seems very
intangible in some sense.

It's like, oh, it's for things
that are really small or really
cold you don't interact with.

But when you actually have these
quantum computers in the lab,
you can play with them.
You get to tinker with them.

You actually can
understand them.
And you can build an intuition.

And it's super fun because then
quantum mechanics becomes just

like another tool
in your toolbox.

And it's a very engaging
problem to work on.

But going to the main thing
that you're asking about,

there's a number of things we
need in order for a quantum
computer to be useful.

We need the hardware and
we need the software.

The history of quantum computing
is quite interesting in that

in the mid-'90s or so, people
came up with these kind

of application use
cases to be like, oh,

if only I had a
quantum computer,
I could do all this cool stuff.

But the hardware was nowhere
close to that use case.

So in some sense,
the hardware really

has limited a lot of the
development of these quantum

computers to be able
to make them performant
enough to be interesting.

That being said,
it's a joint problem

both on bringing
down the resource

costs on the application
and software side

but also building
up the hardware

to be large and performant
enough to meet in the middle

until we really have that first
real world application use case.

SINEAD BOVELL: And
I think we have

started to hear about
progress more frequently.

In the last 18
months, we've been

hearing from multiple companies
different breakthroughs
or milestones.
Sorry.

So for somebody who's
not in the field,

how should we decipher what's
a breakthrough that matters
and what is actually just hype?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.
This is a really good question.

And I'll say, even
for an expert,

it can be difficult to look in
the media and decipher exactly

what is important
and what isn't.
You really dig into the details.

But I can offer maybe
three points of guidance

that people can ask
whenever they're

looking at some
type of announcement

to get a sense of
how important it is.

So number one, at the end of the
day, we're building a computer.

It might be a computer that
runs on quantum mechanics
but it's a computer.
And computers have spec sheets.

They have how big
is your computer.
How fast does it run?

How does the
information move around?
How is the connectivity?

And you can use
this to sort of cut

through these big
hypey announcements

and say how powerful is
the thing that you built.

So for example,
recently in December,

we had this announcement
of our chip Willow.

And we very
deliberately attached

a sort of specification
sheet to be like, here's

all the numbers that
we think that you

need to know to understand what
this computer has the capability
to do.

And that's a guide that
when you see something else,

you can say, OK, well, now
tell me a bit more about it
if someone--

yeah, just help ground
you at the end of the day.

Number two is this
sort of, will it scale?

Some demonstrations are kind
of small foundational things.

But at the end of the day, we
want to build a large scale
computer.

And the third thing is, at the
end of the day, the competition

for a quantum computer really
is a classical supercomputer.

So if you're running something
on your quantum computer,

but you can also do it on
a classical supercomputer,
why should you care?

That really, for us, is
where we feel the bar is.

We need to solve problems that
otherwise would be completely

impossible on our
quantum computers

or we don't think that
we've done our job.
SINEAD BOVELL: I see.
OK.

So now we know
what counts as hype
and to follow that spec sheet.

So Google recently achieved
a significant milestone

in quantum error correction
with its Willow chip.

So can you provide
some context as

to where this places Google in
the global landscape of quantum

computing and why this
breakthrough was quite

pivotal on the journey to
scalable and practical quantum
computers?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

So we're really excited
about this breakthrough
in quantum error correction.

And what we're able to
do with our Willow chip

is meet an outstanding challenge
that's been in the field
for 30 years.

And what that is is that when
the theory of quantum error

correction came out in
the mid-'90s, the thing

that's tricky about it is
you actually have to make

the hardware good enough
in order for it to work.

There's a threshold
that you have to cross.

Otherwise it won't
solve your problem.

And you can't make a
machine that's large scale.

So in terms of maybe
an analogy to make

it a bit more
accessible, imagine

that there are a whole
bunch of companies or teams

trying to get into
the aviation industry.

Suppose no one had ever
built a plane before.

And you might have
a plane over here.

It's like this one's
got two engines.
How cool is that?

This one's got two floors
in, like, first class.
And that one's got a cargo bay.

And everyone's talking
about how we're

going to fly people
all over the world

and do logistics
and stuff like that.

But the planes need to
fly at the end of the day.

They need to be able
to sustain flight.

And in some sense,
this is showing

that Willow, this
chip that we made,

can actually do that in the
context of error correction,

can pass this
threshold of liftoff
and sustaining its flight.

And that's really a foundational
requirement in order

to build and scale up
to larger computers.

SINEAD BOVELL: Really
helpful analogy.
OK.

So in the world of AI,
we hear a lot about AGI.

That is the kind of
Holy Grail moment.

It is the moment where
everything changes.

Is there a particular
moment in quantum

that would be considered
the AGI moment of quantum

where everything changes
from that point forward?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

I think a moment
that you could look

to that would be sort of
an undeniable breakthrough

is that if you can solve
this, a practical, real world

application, and then do that
on an error-corrected computer--

and the reason that
this is so important is

the sort of you've
crossed this 0 to 1

moment of quantum computing
because you've not only

got a real world application
but you've done it

on this foundation that can
then be incrementally improved

and will go into the
future starting up

you could imagine a
virtuous cycle or a Moore's

law for quantum
computing in that case,

be much more predictable, and
understandable, and reliable.

And I think at that point,
it would be undeniable

that this new computing
paradigm is here.

It can do things
that are useful.

And we can see how it's going
to move into the future.

SINEAD BOVELL: And
is there an acronym?

Is there a QGI somewhere
that we can find?

JULIAN KELLY: That's
a great question.
I don't think we have a--

or I'm not aware of a cute
nickname for that yet.
We should probably get one.

SINEAD BOVELL: And so how
can the average person

expect quantum computers
to be a part of their life?

Am I on my laptop and then I
go into a quantum application

or a problem that's unique
for quantum computers

or can we expect the line
of quantum-first devices
in the pipeline?

JULIAN KELLY: The way that we're
thinking about it right now is

that you have to rewind
yourself a number of decades

on the classical
computing front.

We're kind of building, in some
sense, these quantum mainframes

that people would dial into
originally for specialized
problems.

So we talked a bit about
the drug discovery one.

Quantum computing also has
applications in, for example,

optimization is
potential benefit.

There's other cases like
in industrial chemistry,
like fertilizer production.

It could greatly
enhance the process

to make that reduce
the amount of energy.

So the initial use cases
that we have targeted

are very focused on these
big scientific and industrial
questions.

That being said, I think that
if you were to ask someone

in the '50s, or '60s, or
'70s about where you thought

computing was going, I don't
think anyone would have

predicted the type of technology
that you see around us today

or was just shown
off here as well.

So that's a couple
steps in the future.

I don't know of any
super clear use case
to say one way or the other.
But yeah.

I think it's a good
mental model in terms

of where we are in
quantum computing

as compared to looking at the
history of the development
of classical computing.

SINEAD BOVELL: Because famously,
Andy Grove, CEO of Intel,

in 1992 said, the
idea of a smartphone

was nothing more than a
pipe dream fueled by greed.
And now here we are.

JULIAN KELLY: And there's
a nice quote, I think,

from IBM at some point in
the 1900s about we think that

there's only like a desire for
five computers in the entire

world, which is that's clearly
not where we're at today.

SINEAD BOVELL: Meanwhile,
computers driving us around now.
OK.
So a big question.

Are quantum computers going
to break the internet?
And if so-- or break encryption.

And if so, what should every
government and every holder

of Bitcoin be doing
today to prepare
for what experts call Q Day?
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

So I think the kind of
application to encryption

has been something that's
been known for over 30
years at this point.

It was discovered
in the mid-'90s.

And I think the fact
that you're asking me

about it is actually really
great evidence that this is sort
of a well understood issue.

There are government
institutions like NIST,

for example, that are
leading the way in developing

post-quantum
cryptography protocols.

And I think we're
working to support them.
We're collaborating with them.

And I think it's important that
we get those sort of rolled out
to everyone.

That being said, in terms
of maybe an analogy, one

to think about is something like
Y2K where people were really
worried about this.

Some people are
thinking, oh, the planes

are going to fall out of
the sky and the whole world
is going to shut down.

But people are very
aware of the issue.

And then people did all the prep
work to get everything in order.

And then when it
actually came, it
was a bit of a nothing burger.

And it's a little bit of a
butt of jokes at some times.

I mean, you have to do
the preparation for it.

But that's a kind of
example to keep in mind.

SINEAD BOVELL: So December 2029,
if you are holding Bitcoin,
you might be able to still hold.
OK.

So we've established
quantum computers,

they are coming
eventually and that we

do have to do some migration
for the post-quantum world,
the post-world of encryption.

Who is responsible for preparing
the world for this, especially

a problem that spans
governments, industries,
and billions of devices?
Who's actually overseeing this?

JULIAN KELLY: I mean I think
we're really looking, again,

to leadership from
institutions like NIST
to help set the standard.

And then we can support
that in any way that we can

in terms of product
integration, for example,

with things that we've
already started to work on.

But I think that's
where we look to.

SINEAD BOVELL: So it's
kind of in collaboration

with the academic side,
with government side,
and with the private sector.
JULIAN KELLY: That's right.

SINEAD BOVELL: So in a recent
blog post published by Google,

it was written that quantum
computers could one day prove

that we may live
in a multiverse.
So in your opinion, do we?

And if so, what do
quantum scientists know
that the rest of us do not?

JULIAN KELLY: So this
is a very fun question

for quantum physicists that
goes back about 100 years or so.

This is like the 100th year of
quantum that we're celebrating
this year.

And one of the things that
this fundamentally stems from
is that there's this--

it seems in some cases
there's two rules that govern
how quantum systems work.

You just use one
set of equations

and you do like a
measurement at the end.

And we call it the
measurement problem.

And what this does is we
know extremely well how
quantum systems work.

It's one of the best
understood and measured
theories in physics.

It's been tested very
rigorously in all
these different applications.

But there's this
one key point where
it's left up to interpretation.

And so one of the
interpretations

is called the many-worlds
interpretation.

There's the Copenhagen
interpretation.
And there's others as well.

My personal taste is I'm
very much an experimentalist.
And if I want to--

I spend my time
thinking about things

that I can construct a
very clear hypothesis for.

So like until we're
kind of at that moment,

I will say, honestly,
for me personally,

not something I think
about that much,

but I know a lot of
other people really

enjoy the kind of
philosophical debate of what
quantum mechanics means.
SINEAD BOVELL: Yeah.

I think a lot of people inferred
because Willow chip helped

to solve a problem that would
have taken a classical computer

longer than the age of the
universe to solve, well,

therefore, it must
prove that there's
some multiverses going on.

But I guess that's up to the
beholder in terms of how they're
interpreting that.
JULIAN KELLY: Yeah.

I mean, I think it's also just
like another really good test

of quantum mechanics
because, honestly,

before we were able to start
doing these demonstrations,

people still had
these suspicions that,

hey, maybe when you get a really
big, controllable, fine-tuned

quantum system,
you're going to start

to see some deviations
from the underlying theory

That was just sort of a hunch
or a concern that people had.
And we haven't seen that.

In fact, we just
have more evidence

that quantum mechanics works
the way that we think it does,

which is [? really-- ?]
it's pretty interesting.
SINEAD BOVELL: Alrighty.
Thank you so much, Julian.
This was a pleasure.
Thank you all.
[APPLAUSE]
[MUSIC PLAYING]
